EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the western Carolinas during the evening hours. The combination of strong wind shear and weak instability contributed to the development of a broken line of thunderstorms, a couple of which produced QLCS (non-supercell) tornadoes over the Upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damage path was surveyed starting in the Old Market Square area near downtown Easley. Numerous buildings in the downtown area received minor to significant roof damage. Some trees fell on homes as well. The tornado appeared to lift briefly as it traveled north northeast from the downtown area. However, the damage path appeared again in the Lenhardt Rd area just north of downtown. An anchored mobile home was rolled 60 to 80 feet and completely destroyed at this location. Numerous trees were also uprooted or snapped in this area.

By early evening, precipitation had changed to all snow over the upstate, and most areas had experienced a rare white Christmas by late evening. At the Greenville-Spartanburg Airport, it was the first white Christmas since 1962. Over the northwest piedmont precipitation didn���t change over to all snow until shortly after midnight. Snow continued to fall steadily throughout Christmas evening, and upstate locations reported heavy snowfall totals by midnight. The northwest piedmont didn���t see heavy snow totals until shortly before sunrise. By the time the snow tapered off to flurries and light snow showers on the 26th, 2 to 5 inches had fallen across the area.

The snow gradually spread across the rest of the Upstate and northwest Piedmont and became heavier  during the early evening hours.  By mid-evening, accumulations ranged from around 1 to 4 inches across the area. Numerous traffic accidents occurred during the evening rush. The snow tapered off from southwest to northeast across the region a few hours either side of midnight. Total accumulations ranged from around 3 inches across northern portions of the Upstate, to as much as 5 inches across lower portions of the Upstate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low developed over central Georgia during the morning and afternoon hours as a strong shortwave rounded the base of an upper trough. As this low deepened, a warm front developed just north of the Savannah River. South-southest flow behind the developing warm front brought deep moisture back into the region. A few breaks in the cloud cover allowed CAPE values to reach 2000 J/kg before a low level jet and shortwave kicked off showers and thunderstorms. Storms initially became heavy rain producers before crossing the warm front. As thunderstorms crossed the warm front they encountered greater shear allowing them to become severe with signs of rotation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported golf ball size hail covering the ground, 5 miles north-northwest of Crockettville, South Carolina.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Charleston, South Carolina, in conjunction with Beaufort county Emergency Management, confirmed a small EF-0 tornado touched down at 1111 pm on April 8, 2010, two miles west of Bluffton and traveled northeast approximately 0.15 miles before dissipating.  The tornado snapped off the tops of a few pine trees, a few of which landed on and damaged vehicles, tore off some aluminum flashing from homes, knocked down several fences, destroyed a shed and damaged a swing set.  Maximum winds were estimated near 75 mph and the maximum path width was around 20 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak short wave in the mountains shifted southeast and transitioned into a shear |axis that extended from the Midlands of South Carolina into southwest Georgia during the evening. Strong surface heating led to moderate instability along an inland moving sea breeze, which eventually sparked a round of scattered diurnal convection by mid afternoon. High PWAT and DCAPE values supported thunderstorms capable of producing heavy rain and damaging straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage was seen to a hanger at the Charleston International Airport. Airport police also reported minor damage to a jet bridge at the terminal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a weak lee-side trough over the eastern midlands.  The thunderstorms developed and organized into a line as they met up with the inland pushing sea breeze across South Carolina.  Additional thunderstorms developed late across southeast Georgia as outflow boundary collisions occurred in a moist and unstable atmosphere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down and blocking parts of Langfordville Road, 2 miles southwest of Grays, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive and deep layered ridge of high pressure extending across the southeast United States resulted in a very hot and humid airmass across southern South Carolina, with heat index values in excess of 115 degrees in areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heat index value of 116 degrees was observed at the Charleston International Airport in North Charleston, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon along an inland surface trough west and northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms pushed outflow into environment featuring surface based CAPE values of 3500-5000 j/kg over the forecast area. As this occurred new multicell thunderstorms developed and exhibited pulse severe characteristics, especially where boundary interactions occurred. Several thunderstorms produced scattered reports of wind damage mainly along and west of interstate 95 from mid afternoon into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down on Ritter Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record heat across the area along with plenty of moisture produced heat index values between 115 and 125 degrees.  Surface Based CAPE values reached as high as 6000-6500 j/kg with DCAPE values approaching 1500-1600 j/kg.  Thunderstorms initially formed in close proximity to an inland trough of low pressure and along the sea breeze.  The convection sent out numerous outflow boundaries triggering additional thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported power lines down at the intersection of River Road and Swygert Boulevard, 3 miles north-northeast of Johns Island, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy onshore winds resulted in numerous rip currents at South Carolina beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people were caught in a rip current around 12 pm EDT near the pier at Edisto Beach, South Carolina.  Two individuals were able to free themselves from the rip current and swim back to shore, one individual was rescued with injuries and the other drowned.  The drowning victim, James Jordan, was a 19 year old male from Denmark, South Carolina.  The body was recovered around 2 pm EDT by search and rescue officials.  The injured individual was flown by helicopter to the Medical University of South Carolina (MUSC).  The Colleton county, South Carolina Coroner, Richard M. Harvey, confirmed the drowning was the result of a strong rip current near the vicinity of the Edisto Beach pier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moving in off the Atlantic along with the sea breeze in a moist unstable atmosphere, resulted in a couple isolated thunderstorms over southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team, determined that damage occurring near the intersection of Steed Creek Road and Halfway Creek Road, 5 miles west-northwest of Awendaw, South Carolina was a result of straight line winds.  The storm survey team found multiple large pine trees were snapped and uprooted in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak surface high pressure was present over South Carolina and Georgia during the morning and afternoon hours. A high CAPE/low shear environment was present by noon as strong surface heating occurred before clouds developed. Shower and thunderstorm activity initiated along the seabreeze before shifting inland. Once initial activity developed, several outflow boundaries interacted and thus produced additional thunderstorms. Overall, the environment was conducive for downbursts and marginal|hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 4 pine trees and 1 sixteen inch diameter oak tree snapped.

A high CAPE/low shear environment was present by noon as strong surface heating occurred before clouds developed. Shower and thunderstorm activity initiated along the seabreeze before shifting inland. Once initial activity developed, several outflow boundaries interacted and thus produced additional thunderstorms. Overall, the environment was conducive for downbursts and marginal|hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 2 trees down near the Central Avenue area. One tree was down on Old Parsonage Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed during peak heating hours in the afternoon as a broad trough of low pressure shifted over the Midlands. A hot and humid air mass remained through the evening which allowed convection to persist and shift east over portions of southeast South Carolina. DCAPES between 1200 to 1500 J/KG suggested the potential of damaging winds with any strong thunderstorm that continued into the early overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid atmosphere was present under Atlantic high pressure. A lee side trough developed over the Midlands in South Carolina before shifting east towards the Atlantic Ocean. Forcing from this trough was enough to promote a few strong thunderstorms with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 knot wind gust was measured at an observation site 2 miles north northwest of Eadytown on the south side of the Lake Marion Spillway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable atmosphere was present as hot and humid conditions led to surface based CAPE values between 3000 to 3500 J/KG and low level lapse rates of  between 8 to 9 degrees Celsius. Inverted-v soundings and DCAPES between 1000 to 1200 J/KG suggested thunderstorms capable of producing strong downbursts as strong heating occurred during the afternoon and evening hours. Storms first began to initiate during the afternoon along an inland moving seabreeze. The focus then switched to inland areas where a Midland trough was approaching the southeast coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The police reported one tree down on Wilburn Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, mixed with rain at times, developed over the piedmont and foothills of South Carolina around sunrise. Despite bursts of moderate to heavy snow, a warm ground and above freezing temperatures caused much of the snow to melt upon impact. As a result, accumulations were light, ranging from trace amounts across southern piedmont areas, to an inch or so along the I-85 corridor.

Three quarter horses were killed by a lightning strike at a horse stable near Wiliamston.

Snow began to fall during the pre-dawn across the mountains of South Carolina. After sunrise, snow became moderate to heavy at times, resulting in accumulations of 1 to 4 inches across most of the area by late morning. Snow, heavy at times, continued into the afternoon across the mountains, with heavy accumulations realized in most areas by early afternoon. By early evening, total snowfall ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the area, with localized amounts as high as 10 inches, especially in the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow overspread the region allowing tropical moisture originating in the Caribbean Sea to stream into southern South Carolina.  A developing area of low pressure over the nearby Atlantic Coastal Waters and a deepening upper level low west of the region, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported that one lane of Folly Field Road was closed due to water on the roadway on Hilton Head Island, South Carolina.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported that Pope Avenue and Sea Pine Circle were closed due to high water blocking the roadways, on Hilton Head, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and unstable atmosphere ahead of an approaching cold front, supported the development of scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.  Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in regions where multiple boundary collisions occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse surface front lifted across the area followed by two mid-level short waves. One short wave passed just north of the area while the other passed west and south of the area. These two impulses provided the necessary dynamics to overcome the weakly capped environment. The convection organized into a north-south line and moved across the area during the late afternoon and early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the Upstate during the afternoon hours along a stationary front. A few of the storms became severe, with a particularly intense storm affecting the city of Anderson. This was the second time in June that a significant severe storm affected the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in and around the city, including the downtown area, with a tree on a home on Marshall Ave, and another on a home on Riley street about 4 miles south southwest of the city center. A turret on top of a building was damaged near downtown and the tin roof of another building was damaged. A tree was also blown down at the beginning of the microburst path on Whitehall Rd  about 4 miles northwest of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed over the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia as strong wind shear and moderate instability overspread the region ahead of a strong cold front. A number of tornadoes were spawned by the storms. Only weak tornadoes affected the Upstate, though two strong tornadoes occurred in the western piedmont and foothills of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down near highway 11 near the Pleasant Ridge County Park, where the tops of numerous small trees were snapped off and several large trees were uprooted. The intermittent damage path continued east northeast, crossing highway 25 just north of the highway 11 junction. Damage again appeared near the intersection of Chinquapin Rd and highway 11, where the tops of several trees were snapped off along a ridge line. The most significant damage was observed just east/northeast of this location along Look Up Lodge Rd. Numerous small trees and large limbs were snapped off and several large trees were uprooted. Some minor structural damage was observed due to falling trees and limbs. Some boat docks were flipped on Chinquapin Lake as well. Sporadic tree damage continued to the east northeast, with the tornado apparently lifting between Chestnut Mountain Rd and Old Highway 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of widespread rain moved across Upstate South Carolina ahead of a cold front. A few convective elements developed within the convective line. One of these produced a non-Supercell tornado over the southern part of the Upstate. On radar, the storm exhibited a fairly classic Broken-S tornado signature, which is sometimes associated with tornadoes in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a mobile home on Fuller St in Coronaca, causing major damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the western Carolinas during the evening hours. The combination of strong wind shear and weak instability contributed to the development of a broken line of thunderstorms, a couple of which produced QLCS (non-supercell) tornadoes over the Upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down along Wynfield Court north of Gray Court and moved along an intermittent path in a north northeast direction. Some trees were snapped and an outbuilding overturned near the beginning of the damage path. The tornado skipped to Robert Harris Ct, tearing the underskirting off a mobile home. Damage intensified toward Bramlett Church Rd, where a mobile home was moved from its foundation and had some siding and underskirting removed. Some shingles were torn off homes on Harris Bridge Rd, while the roofs of a well house and storage shed were blown off as well. The tornado lifted near Christmastree Lane, where several large oak trees were uprooted, one of which fell on and damaged a home. Also, a detached garage was completely destroyed at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across northern Florida and then northeastward off the Georgia and South Carolina coast. Precipitation initially fell in the form of rain, but quickly changed over to snow in the late afternoon and evening hours as winds shifted to the north and allowed colder air to wrap back into the region.  Heavy snow accumulated across all of southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department personnel reported 7.0 inches of snow accumulation in Smoaks, South Carolina.  The heavy wet snow in the trees was resulting in the tree limbs breaking.  Roads were reported to be nearly impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across northern Florida and then northeastward off the Georgia and South Carolina coast. Precipitation initially fell in the form of rain, but quickly changed over to snow in the late afternoon and evening hours as winds shifted to the north and allowed colder air to wrap back into the region.  Heavy snow accumulated across all of southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter measured 7.0 inches of snow accumulation in Ridgeville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and Midlands and spawned several tornadoes.  Many homes were damaged in Lexington county with several others in Edgefield county.  Numerous trees were also taken down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 with max winds of 125mph touched down near Ridge Road and continued northeast to Lake Murray.  Around 40 homes had some form of damage with numerous trees and powerlines down.  Several vehicles were also damaged and there was one minor injury. One horse was killed when a portion of a barn collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and Midlands and spawned several tornadoes.  Many homes were damaged in Lexington county with several others in Edgefield county.  Numerous trees were also taken down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado took down many trees along its path from the Sumter National Forest to just southwest of Edgefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several supercell thunderstorms moved through the CSRA and Midlands and spawned several tornadoes.  Many homes were damaged in Lexington county with several others in Edgefield county.  Numerous trees were also taken down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado touched down on hwy 230 near Stevens Creek and took down numerous trees and powerlines.  Two homes had moderate damage and a mobile home had half of its roof torn off.  A motor home was also crushed from a tree falling on it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system that brought a mix of heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain to the mountains of the Upstate also brought heavy rain to the lower elevations. The rain fall for such a long period of time before flooding began that none of it was classified as a flash flood. Two day rainfall amounts ranged from 3 to 4 inches, with a little less over the southern part of the Upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the South Pacolet River exceeded established flood stage during mid-afternoon, indicating that a portion of highway 14 was flooded in the Landrum area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as light snow early in the evening across northern portions of the Upstate. By late evening, snow had mostly changed to sleet, except in areas along the North Carolina border. By the pre-dawn hours, total accumulations ranged from trace amounts of sleet near the I-85 corridor, to as much as 4 inches of snow along the North Carolina border. ||The wintry mix persisted across northern portions of the Upstate overnight. In most areas, accumulations of 1-2 inches of sleet and snow (mainly sleet) occurred, with a tenth to a quarter inch glaze of ice occurring on top of the sleet and snow.  However, a small area of northern Spartanburg County saw more in the way of snow, generally 4-6 inches. Also, a small area of heavy ice accumulation occurred over the mountains of Greenville County, resulting in numerous downed trees and power outages.||Farther to the south, mixture of rain, snow, and sleet developed over the southern part of the Upstate and the northwest Piedmont of South Carolina. The precipitation continued off and on through the overnight, before changing to light rain or freezing rain during the morning of the 30th. Total sleet and snowfall accumulation ranged from trace amounts over the lower piedmont to a half inch closer to I-85. Light ice accretion occurred on the 30th, mainly on elevated surfaces.

The rain fall for such a long period of time before flooding began that none of it was classified as a flash flood. Two day rainfall amounts ranged from 3 to 4 inches, with a little less over the southern part of the Upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding developed in the northern part of the county along portions of the Middle|Tyger, with Mount Lebanon Rd under water for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system tracked across northern Florida and then northeastward off the Georgia and South Carolina coast. Precipitation initially fell in the form of rain, but quickly changed over to snow in the late afternoon and evening hours as winds shifted to the north and allowed colder air to wrap back into the region.  Heavy snow accumulated across all of southern South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports around the southern portion of Colleton county indicated that between 1 and 3 inches of snow accumulated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over the South Carolina mountains during the late afternoon, and continued through the evening and overnight hours. The snow mixed with sleet during the evening, before changing to mostly sleet after midnight, then changing to mainly rain as temperatures aloft warmed during the pre-dawn hours. Total accumulations of sleet and snow ranged from trace amounts near highway 11, to 3 inches or so in elevations above 2000 feet. ||A narrow area near the North Carolina border never warmed above freezing. In this part of the mountains, freezing rain fell through the afternoon on the 5th, and damaging ice accumulations developed by mid-morning. Numerous trees were brought down in this narrow zone within a mile or so of the North Carolina border.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff dispatch reported trees down along Hwy. 23 near the McCormick and Edgefield County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over the eastern Midlands and produced large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm produced an EF1 tornado in northwest Fairfield county.  Many trees were taken down and minor damage was done to a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey followed up on reports of trees down from the utility company and postal carriers.  Many trees were down along the path and minor damage was done to a home and out-buildings at 1517 Clowney road.  The tornado peaked about a quarter of a mile west of the home as an EF1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind damage along a bow echo taking down trees and powerlines in McCormick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported several trees and powerlines down west of McCormick between Hugenot Parlway and Pierce Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along a surface boundary during the evening. The thunderstorm developed supercell characteristics soon after moving into Darlington County and spawned several tornadoes over its lifetime.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team confirmed an EF1 tornado with winds up to 100 mph. A tornado briefly touched down along South Charleston Road. The tornado uprooted or snapped about a dozen hardwood trees, some as large as 18 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold pool associated with an area of low pressure aloft and surface heating steepened lapse rates across the area. An approaching shortwave helped to develop thunderstorms west of Interstate 95 during the mid afternoon. These thunderstorms quickly intensified as they moved east at up to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team confirmed a microburst with winds to 95 mph. The most significant structural damage occurred to a small three-bay auto garage on Juniper Street near Highway 9 in Clio. This brick and cinder block structure lost its sheet metal roof just before the exterior walls collapsed. Two people took shelter inside the building just before the storm hit and were not injured. The roof of the building was lifted and blown approximately 100 yards. A snapped power pole appeared to be the result of the airborne roof. Trees were also down. The damage pattern was indicative of a strong and narrow microburst. The microburst was estimated to be 20 yards wide and 100 yards long.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along a surface boundary during the evening. The thunderstorm developed supercell characteristics soon after moving into Darlington County and spawned several tornadoes over its lifetime.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team confirmed an EF2 tornado with winds estimated to 115 mph. The tornado first touched down near Syracuse Street and Rogers Road. An unanchored double-wide mobile home was rolled off its foundation and onto its side. Several trees along Syracuse Street were uprooted or snapped or had large branches broken off. Several homes suffered minor to moderate shingle damage, largely as a result of fallen trees or branches. The damage was rated EF1 with winds to 90 mph. After briefly lifting, the tornado touched down as it approached Highway 401 and caused major damage to a large steel building. Two bay doors were blown in and a large portion of the roof was lifted and blown off. Sheet metal from the roof was strewn for about 200 yards. A small wooden garage and building were destroyed before the tornado lifted near Highway 401. The damage was rated EF1 with winds to 100 mph. The tornado touched down one last time between Lightly Street and Southern Pine Street. Numerous hardwood trees were uprooted or snapped. A few of these trees, with diameters up to 24 inches, were snapped a few feet from the ground. There was significant damage to some residential homes, primarily the result of falling trees or branches. The tornado continued across Highway 52 business where several businesses and structures sustained minor to moderate damage. A set of car wash bays were blown over and a church lost many shingles and a small portion of its roof. The tornado then continued east of Highway 52 business through a residential neighborhood. Several large trees up to 20 inches in diameter were uprooted. A large section of roof was blown off a home. Cain Elementary School suffered significant damage. Many awnings at the school were damaged or destroyed and one building lost half its roof. Shortly after causing the roof damage to Cain Elementary, the tornado caused more tree damage as it lifted through the treetops. As these trees or their branches fell, several residences sustained minor to moderate damage to include some roof shingle damage. The damage was rated EF2 with winds to 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed along a surface boundary during the evening. The thunderstorm developed supercell characteristics soon after moving into Darlington County and spawned several tornadoes over its lifetime.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team confirmed an EF2 tornado with winds estimated to 130 mph. The tornado first touched down in a stand of pine trees, snapping approximately 30 of them several feet off the ground. The largest of the snapped trees was 12 inches in diameter. The tornado then moved east into a cluster of homes and mobile homes along East 7 Pines Street. One mobile home was blown 20 yards off its frame and was completely destroyed. The debris was blown into another single wide mobile home which was subsequently destroyed. A large pecan tree was uprooted before the tornado lifted off the ground. The damage was rated EF1 with winds to 100 mph. The second touchdown occurred a few hundred yards to the east. The tornado moved over a stand of trees and descended practically on top of a two story single family residence located on East 7 Pines Street. This dwelling was blown off its foundation and flipped over onto two automobiles and in the process was completely destroyed. Three people were in the home at the time. They took shelter in an interior hallway moments before the tornado struck. They were transported to the hospital, treated for minor injuries and released. A Ford Ranger Pickup was lifted and dropped approximately 50 yards away. A horse trailer was lifted and dropped 100 yards away. A large wood framed workshop and horse barn were also destroyed. The tornado moved east approximately two tenths of a mile and damaged or uprooted several trees as it lifted. The damage was rated EF2 with winds to 130 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40 x 60 foot section of roof was blown off a large metal warehouse of a Flakeboard MDF Plant on Highway 912. A trailer was blown off its foundation at the plant. The rear window of a pickup truck was shattered. One person was trapped between a trailer and metal beam and suffered a broken arm. National Weather Service personnel estimated a wind gust to 95 mph caused the damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar...rain gauge data and stream gage data along with a NWS survey, public and media reports indicated flash flooding in the Rocky Branch Creek Basin. Rainfall amounts from 2 to nearly 2.50 inches in one hour were reported in the vicinity of the 5 Pts and Whaley Mill areas of Columbia. Many businesses and some roads were flooding during the event. The Rocky Branch Creek at Whaley St reached 11.2 feet, which is 4.0 feet above the flood stage of 7.2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as light snow early in the evening across northern portions of the Upstate. By late evening, snow had mostly changed to sleet, except in areas along the North Carolina border. By the pre-dawn hours, total accumulations ranged from trace amounts of sleet near the I-85 corridor, to as much as 4 inches of snow along the North Carolina border. ||The wintry mix persisted across northern portions of the Upstate overnight. In most areas, accumulations of 1-2 inches of sleet and snow (mainly sleet) occurred, with a tenth to a quarter inch glaze of ice occurring on top of the sleet and snow.  However, a small area of northern Spartanburg County saw more in the way of snow, generally 4-6 inches. Also, a small area of heavy ice accumulation occurred over the mountains of Greenville County, resulting in numerous downed trees and power outages.||Farther to the south, mixture of rain, snow, and sleet developed over the southern part of the Upstate and the northwest Piedmont of South Carolina. The precipitation continued off and on through the overnight, before changing to light rain or freezing rain during the morning of the 30th. Total sleet and snowfall accumulation ranged from trace amounts over the lower piedmont to a half inch closer to I-85. Light ice accretion occurred on the 30th, mainly on elevated surfaces.

Widespread heavy rain affected the region ahead of a slow moving cold front. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were common across Upstate South Carolina. Quite a bit of flash flooding developed as a result of the heavy rain falling on already saturated ground. An isolated severe storm also downed several trees in Oconee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended across the county shortly after midnight, runoff from the earlier rainfall caused flooding to persist across the eastern half of the county until after sunrise.

Roads affected by these flooding streams include Mount Gallant Rd, West Oak Rd, McConnels Highway, and Ogden Rd.

The Reedy River exceeded its established flood stage in downtown Greenville by 2 feet. Closed roads included Boiling Springs Rd at Sugar Creek Rd, Garlington Rd at I-385 and Poplar St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain affected the region ahead of a slow moving cold front. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were common across Upstate South Carolina. Quite a bit of flash flooding developed as a result of the heavy rain falling on already saturated ground. An isolated severe storm also downed several trees in Oconee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended across the area by midnight, runoff from earlier rainfall caused high water conditions to persist across the county through the rest of the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain affected the region ahead of a slow moving cold front. Rainfall amounts of 2.5 to 4 inches were common across Upstate South Carolina. Quite a bit of flash flooding developed as a result of the heavy rain falling on already saturated ground. An isolated severe storm also downed several trees in Oconee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended across the area around midnight, runoff from the earlier rainfall caused flooding to persist across portions of Greenville County through the rest of the night. Flooding along the Reedy River persisted into the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as light snow early in the evening across northern portions of the Upstate. By late evening, snow had mostly changed to sleet, except in areas along the North Carolina border. By the pre-dawn hours, total accumulations ranged from trace amounts of sleet near the I-85 corridor, to as much as 4 inches of snow along the North Carolina border. ||The wintry mix persisted across northern portions of the Upstate overnight. In most areas, accumulations of 1-2 inches of sleet and snow (mainly sleet) occurred, with a tenth to a quarter inch glaze of ice occurring on top of the sleet and snow.  However, a small area of northern Spartanburg County saw more in the way of snow, generally 4-6 inches. Also, a small area of heavy ice accumulation occurred over the mountains of Greenville County, resulting in numerous downed trees and power outages.||Farther to the south, mixture of rain, snow, and sleet developed over the southern part of the Upstate and the northwest Piedmont of South Carolina. The precipitation continued off and on through the overnight, before changing to light rain or freezing rain during the morning of the 30th. Total sleet and snowfall accumulation ranged from trace amounts over the lower piedmont to a half inch closer to I-85. Light ice accretion occurred on the 30th, mainly on elevated surfaces.

A complex of thunderstorms moved into the area from the north during the late morning and early afternoon. Daytime heating and a mid level cold pool steepened the lapse rates during the mid and late afternoon and this allowed thunderstorms to intensify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported on Cypress Drive. The hail lasted for about 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thundersrtorms across the CWA produced wet microburst with wind gust to 80mph taking down trees and powerlines.  Large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employese reported 1 inch hail in the vicinity of Ball Park road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thundersrtorms across the CWA produced wet microburst with wind gust to 80mph taking down trees and powerlines.  Large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees down from Percival road at Smallwood Court to Screaming Eagle and Kelly Mill  roads.  A tree also fell on a car at Claremont and Kilbourne roads crushing it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection initiated along a stalled backdoor front during the late afternoon. The thunderstorms quickly intensified in a strongly unstable environment. The cells initially produced hail, but as cold pools developed, cell motions accelerated and resulted in damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters was reported at the Lighthouse Restaurant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection initiated along a stalled backdoor front during the late afternoon. The thunderstorms quickly intensified in a strongly unstable environment. The cells initially produced hail, but as cold pools developed, cell motions accelerated and resulted in damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of roof (9 x 30 feet), was torn off a building on Bethlehem Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cluster severe storms produced microburt wind damage across portions of the Midlands taking down trees and powerlines.  Some homes were also damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported trees and powerlines down. Three roofs on buildings heavily damaged at the public works facility and a couple of trees were on homes on Jerry Street and Hwy 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Atlantic high pressure and an inland trough were set in place during the overnight hours. A shortwave and persistent instability continued to support showers and thunderstorms overnight in a corridor north of Interstate 16 and west of Interstate 26. Zero to six kilometer bulk shear between 20 to 30 knots supported multicell thunderstorms. Downdraft CAPE between 500 to 1000 joules per kilogram also supported brief wind gusts from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported one tree down on Broad River Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of thunderstorms intensified lee of the Appalachians during the late afternoon. The cold pool associated with these thunderstorms gusted out ahead of the storms and accelerated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree fell onto power lines. The tree and power lines fell across the roadway, blocking Judson Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection fired along the lee side trough during the afternoon. Thunderstorms intensified during the late afternoon as they moved southeast into an area of higher instability. A lone severe thunderstorm did move to the right of the mean flow for a period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down on Chair Road. The tree was blocking the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low off the southeast coast triggered scattered thunderstorms during the late evening hours over the Upstate. The storms produced a little hail and quite a bit of wind damage in Greenwood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from the Hodges and Cokesbury area south to near Greenwood. The hardest hit areas were in the vicinity Clifton St, Pope St, Deadfall Rd, and Carlton St. Two trees fell on and damaged homes on Carlton St. Numerous power outages also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Atlantic high pressure and an inland trough were set in place during the overnight hours. Zero to six kilometer bulk shear between 20 to 30 knots supported multicell thunderstorms. Downdraft CAPE between 500 to 1000 joules per kilogram also supported brief wind gusts from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported one tree down on Dr. Martin Luther King Drive on Saint Helena Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance in combination with the sea breeze in an unstable environment resulted in numerous thunderstorms developing across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a tree down near the intersection of Ritter Road and Blue House Lane, 2 miles southeast of Hendersonville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind shear and moderate instability developed over the region ahead of a cold front. Several severe storms affected Upstate South Carolina during the late afternoon and evening hours. Large hail was the most commonly reported severe weather, though one supercell thunderstorm produced two tornadoes starting just over the state line up in the western piedmont of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported on Old Landfill Rd, about 7.5 miles northwest of Abbeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of showers and thunderstorms moved north, across the Upstate, during the nighttime hours. One storm in the line briefly became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Riverfork Rd and another on Cannon Rd about 4 miles west southwest of Waterloo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of showers and thunderstorms moved north, across the Upstate, during the nighttime hours. One storm in the line briefly became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
A strong cold front crossed the region during the evening hours, triggering severe thunderstorms and a couple non-supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short tornado track was surveyed in the Fair Play community. The tornado touched down along highway 59 between Cleveland Creek Rd and Doctors Rd. Part of the roof was blown off a manufactured home at this location, and numerous trees were snapped off or uprooted. The tornado continued in a northeast direction toward the Pine Grove Rd area, where damage to trees continued near the intersection of Grant Rd. The tornado appeared to dissipate in this area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along highway 187 near Lake Hartwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low triggered scattered afternoon thunderstorms over Upstate South Carolina. The storms produced hail of various sizes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near Old York Rd and Darby Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low triggered scattered afternoon thunderstorms over Upstate South Carolina. The storms produced hail of various sizes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down near highway 97 and Ross Dye Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the region during the evening hours, triggering severe thunderstorms and a couple non-supercell tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damage path began in the Harper Rd area of Pendleton. A large detached garage had its windward-facing doors blown in. This caused a large cargo door on the back side of the garage to blow out. Several horse stables on Harper Rd had metal roofs partially peeled back or blown off. Numerous trees were snapped off in this area, with a few large trees uprooted. The tornado continued northeast to Liberty Highway, just south of the Cherry St extension. The metal roofing was partially torn off a barn at this location, and several outbuildings were damaged or blown away. A large, well-constructed home received minor eaves damage. The tornado turned in a more east northeasterly direction at this point, dissipating shortly after crossing Liberty Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low triggered scattered afternoon thunderstorms over Upstate South Carolina. The storms produced hail of various sizes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported at the intersection of highway 211 and Garner Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms around the Midlands produced locally heavy rains and microbursts that took down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported many trees down around N. Augusta.  Several trailers were damaged on Tigerlilly Drive and Dafodill Lane from trees falling onto them.  Several homes on Yardly Drive also had trees fall onto them.  A well secured radio tower was bent over as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between four and six inches of rain fell, flooding downtown Hemingway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree, which fell on a trailer, causing extensive damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near the Piedmont trough in a strongly unstable airmass.  Steep low level lapse rates were instrumental in bringing strong to damaging wind gusts to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced some wind damage around the county warning area.  Heavy rains if Fairfield county also sent some streams over their banks causing minor flooding of low lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported a couple of trees down on Springfield road and Buffalo Pond road near Capital Hwy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between four and six inches of rain fell, flooding downtown Hemingway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey by a NWS employee revealed extensive flooding in downtown Hemingway. The back parking lot of the Post Office flooded, with water reaching the floorboards of several vans.  A dumpster floated to the other side of the parking lot.  Water flooded the Masonic Temple, despite sandbags.  Flood waters also entered the Town Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated convection developed along the seabreeze in Horry County and slowly propagated southward along this boundary. One thunderstorm did briefly strengthen to severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree was reported down at the South Carolina National Guard at the intersection of South Kings Highway and 19th Avenue S. Signs were blown out at the Big Lots store at the intersection of South Kings Highway and 14th Avenue South. A fabric awning was also ripped from the store front. Several Bradford Pear trees were snapped and numerous limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms along a frontal boundary produced flooding in the city of Florence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding in downtown Florence.  Law enforcement reported that Church Street at James Jones Blvd, as well as the underpasses at Palmetto Street and Cheves Street were all under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed along a pre-frontal trough during the mid afternoon and persisted into the early evening. The thunderstorms organized into line segments and intensified to severe levels given the very unstable environment and very steep mid level lapse rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs were reported down along Georgetown Road. The limbs had a diameter of 8 to 10  inches. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms took down some trees with microbursts and also produced flash flooding in Columbia.  Heavy rains also produced flooding in some low lying areas around the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USGS and City Police reported flooding in downtown Columbia in the Five Points area and also the USC campus near Main and Whaley streets.  Water levels were up to 4 feet deep in some areas and spilled over into some apartments and also Walgreens in Five Points.  The Rocky Branch Creek gage crested at 10.7 feet which is 3.5 feet above flood stage.  Several vehicles became trapped when they tried to drive through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low off the southeast coast triggered scattered thunderstorms during the late evening hours over the Upstate. The storms produced a little hail and quite a bit of wind damage in Greenwood County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Salak Rd area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low off the southeast coast triggered scattered thunderstorms during the late evening hours over the Upstate. The storms produced a little hail and quite a bit of wind damage in Greenwood County.
Numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours along a warm front over Upstate South Carolina. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Hayes Rd and McKenzie Dr about 5 miles south southeast of Anderson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours along a warm front over Upstate South Carolina. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down on the southeast side of Anderson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and large limbs were blown down on Barclay Ave. A tree fell on a home near the Beech St intersection. Other trees were blown down along New St and Oneal St.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the Blacksburg area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Stone Rd about 6 miles southwest of Fork Shoals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours along a warm front over Upstate South Carolina. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Dunklin Bridge Rd and McKelvey Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms in the Midlands produced wind damage and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported numerous trees down and minor damage to a roof and other out buildings from Red Hill road and Hwy 441 to Green Lane, Williamson Road, Rambert Church Road, and US 15.  The downburst wind damage was widespread and lasted eight minutes with a relatively slow moving thunderstorm that also produced a weak EF0 tornado just west of the downburst area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and multiple large limbs, up to 5 inches in diameter, were blown down on Shackleburg Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail fell near the intersection of highways 18 and 105.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multicellular thunderstorms produced many microbursts and a few tornadoes across central and north central South Carolina.  Many trees were taken down and about two dozen homes had minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS and Emergency Management found around two dozen homes with minor damage and numerous trees down along Camp Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced some large hail and damaging winds across much of the county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Dept. reported trees and powerlines down, roof damage to several structures, and some storage buildings destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure began to weaken as a pre-frontal trough lingered over the inland Carolinas. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening hours as an inland moving sea breeze interacted with differential heating boundaries. A few thunderstorms became strong with marginal hail and damaging winds as they entered an environment that had surface based CAPE values around 3000 j/kg and DCAPE values over 800 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on Route 52 within the Saint Stephen city limits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread surface heating and continued deep moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front from the northwest allowed for very unstable atmospheric conditions with CAPE values between 3500 to 4000 J/kg Friday afternoon and evening. As a 500 mb shortwave tracked along the southern periphery of a broad through and across the region, severe thunderstorms developed across the area. Severe thunderstorms initially developed along the seabreeze in the Charleston area late in the afternoon. More organized severe storms tracked into the area from the Midlands during the evening and pushed all the way to the coast, with the strongest storms across southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The broadcast media relayed a report of a funnel cloud from a viewer in Goose Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe storms moved into the Upstate and northwest piedmont during the mid afternoon hours. Additional storms developed over the western Upstate along merging outflow boundaries from earlier storms. A particularly intense storm affected Anderson on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in the area near highway 11 and Ivey Rd. One tree fell on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure began to weaken as a pre-frontal trough lingered over the inland Carolinas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1 to 3 feet pieces of siding blown off a house. A 12 feet canoe was also blown across the yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the Upstate during the afternoon hours along a stationary front. A few of the storms became severe, with a particularly intense storm affecting the city of Anderson. This was the second time in June that a significant severe storm affected the city.
A complex of thunderstorms developed over the western part of the Upstate with some of the storms producing wind damage. A more isolated severe storm caused wind damage over Spartanburg County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was brought down on power lines on Beverly Dr about 4 miles north of town. Several large branches were brought down in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over western South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. With moderate wind shear over the region, a few multicell severe storms developed which produced wind damage and some small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of downed trees were received from the Georgia border to Ricks|Rd to the I-85/highway 11 intersection. A boat dock on Lake Hartwell was overturned in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a surface trough which was stationary across the Carolinas during the afternoon. The environment was strongly unstable and weakly sheared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree was reported down on a house along South Center Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front was stalled to the north of the area during the afternoon. Slow moving thunderstorms developed along a strong and fast moving seabreeze and became widespread as more thunderstorms developed along multiple outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large tree was reported down along Juniper Bay Road. A fence was also reported blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled across the county warning area producing more wet microburst that took down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported trees down on I-26 near mile marker 167.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over western South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. With moderate wind shear over the region, a few multicell severe storms developed which produced wind damage and some small hail.

Atlantic high pressure dominated the southeast U.S., creating a typical seabreeze convection event during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Southwest flow pinned the seabreeze along the south carolina coast, thus allowing thunderstorms to develop over coastal counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported one tree down blocking south okatie highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage to a house. A billboard sustained damage. Several trees were reported down. A trampoline was picked up and blown downwind. Field corn was also reported knocked down. The damage was along a stretch of Bobo Newsom Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moved in from the north over portions of southeast South Carolina. This provided enhanced lift in a very moist and unstable atmosphere over the forecast area. As thunderstorms developed they became clustered and pulsed to produce isolated severe winds. Heavy rains fell in and around the Charleston metro near high tide, thus enhancing flooding to prone locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down and was blocking Pinckney Colony Road near the Fording Island Road intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front over Upstate South Carolina during the afternoon hours. Some of the storms produced damaging straight-line winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees were blown down near the intersection of Klugh Rd and Dungannon Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front over Upstate South Carolina during the afternoon hours. Some of the storms produced damaging straight-line winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the Pelham Rd area from I-85 to highway 14, continuing south to the Woodruff Rd area. One tree damaged a porch on Radcliffe Rd, about 6 miles north of Simpsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow overspread the region allowing tropical moisture originating in the Caribbean Sea to stream into southern South Carolina.  A developing area of low pressure over the nearby Atlantic Coastal Waters and a deepening upper level low west of the region, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement on Hilton Head Island, South Carolina, reported the intersection of William Hilton Parkway and New Orleans Road was closed due to high water on the roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reported that the intersection of King Street and Huger Street were flooded with water roughly 1 to 1.5 feet deep due to heavy rainfall at the time of high tide.  The road remained open, but many cars attempting to push through the high water stalled out and were unable to make it through.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a fire at the Golden Corral in Bluffton, South Carolina. Firefighters found smoke and flames coming from the roof. The fire was quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was present over South Carolina as an area of low pressure remained west of the county warning area with a weak surface front extending east along the Savannah River. Precipitable water values approached 2.5 inches as tropical moisture continued to advect over the region from the south during the late morning into early afternoon hours. As an early afternoon sea breeze developed, high precipitable water values contributed to several showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported street flooding on Long Point Road near Boone Hall and on Coleman Blvd near the Moultrie Middle School. Police were standing by to block both areas. Firefighters were also standing by water that was entering a residence at 526 Whilden Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near mile marker 57 on I-85 and another was downed on Abner Creek Rd in the same area. Also, a gust of 51 mph was measured at GSP airport during this time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in the Inglewood Dr area, a couple miles north of downtown Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass created surface based CAPE values near 4500 J/kg and lifted indices near -8 during peak heating hours. Thunderstorms initiated during the evening hours as a stationary front just to the north of the county warning area interacted with a sea breeze. Numerous outflow boundaries from initial thunderstorm activity produced additional thunderstorms through the early overnight hours. Some thunderstorms produced damaging winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2 inch diameter limbs down in North Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm took down some large limbs which brought powerlines down and caused some local outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind from a gravity wave was recorded at the Georgetown County Airport.  Strong winds from the wave were also observed in Myrtle Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 mph wind gust was recorded at the Georgetown County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably mild and humid airmass spread across the southeast states ahead of a deep trough which was located over the middle part of the country. A nocturnal MCS developed in this mild air and pushed into the western Carolinas during the morning hours. As the line crossed the region, it produced several areas of wind damage, mainly across the North Carolina mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down on Norman Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached from the west during the afternoon.  The freezing level dropped through the day as colder air moved in aloft.  Thunderstorms developed right ahead of the front and intensified.  There were several reports of large hail during the late afternoon.  Hail to the size of golf balls was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An expansive and deep layered ridge of high pressure extending across the southeast United States resulted in very hot and humid conditions across southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia, with heat index values in excess of 115 degrees in couple areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread surface heating and continued deep moisture advection ahead of an approaching cold front from the northwest allowed for very unstable atmospheric conditions with CAPE values between 3500 to 4000 J/kg Friday afternoon and evening. As a 500 mb shortwave tracked along the southern periphery of a broad through and across the region, severe thunderstorms developed across the area. Severe thunderstorms initially developed along the seabreeze in the Charleston area late in the afternoon. More organized severe storms tracked into the area from the Midlands during the evening and pushed all the way to the coast, with the strongest storms across southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on Vandross Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse surface front lifted across the area followed by two mid-level short waves. One short wave passed just north of the area while the other passed west and south of the area. These two impulses provided the necessary dynamics to overcome the weakly capped environment. The convection organized into a north-south line and moved across the area during the late afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate roof damage was sustained to a large, old building along Highway 52. The building was being utilized to store furniture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was draped northwest to southeast across the area during the evening. The seabreeze boundary also sharpened as it began to move inland. Thunderstorms were fueled by a highly unstable and moisture rich environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down across Valley Forge Road, near the intersection with Green Sea Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak frontal boundary aided by a weak shortwave trough, and advanced eastward into an unstable and weakly sheared environment over southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported lightning entered their home through the chimney and struck a teenage boy, 1 mile north-northwest of Shadowmoss Plantation in the Charleston, South Carolina metropolitan area.  Emergency Medical Technicians treated the boy at the home where he was struck by the lightning.  Newspaper and print media reported, The teen likely experienced the effects of a flash.  A flash happens when a person is near an electrical current, but not actually hit with it.  The proximity to the current can result in anything from tingling to actually knocking a person off their feet.  Several Charleston-based news agencies have talked to the teen who said the lightning traveled down the chimney and came in through the fireplace before striking him in the thigh.  After the flash, the teen reported having a tingling sensation in his leg.  EMS officials said there were no burns or damage around the home and the tingling sensation the teen reported is consistent with a flash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid and upper level ridge axis weakened as it shifted south during the early afternoon hours. Strong surface heating resulted in CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over the region. As the seabreeze began to shift inland a few strong thunderstorms developed. A nearly stationary frontal boundary became the focus of severe thunderstorms over the Midlands during the mid afternoon.  As this frontal boundary shifted southward a line of severe thunderstorms swept through portions of southern South Carolina and southeast Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind shear and moderate instability developed over the region ahead of a cold front. Several severe storms affected Upstate South Carolina during the late afternoon and evening hours. Large hail was the most commonly reported severe weather, though one supercell thunderstorm produced two tornadoes starting just over the state line up in the western piedmont of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail slightly larger than quarter size was reported near the intersection of highways 296 and 290.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind shear and moderate instability developed over the region ahead of a cold front. Several severe storms affected Upstate South Carolina during the late afternoon and evening hours. Large hail was the most commonly reported severe weather, though one supercell thunderstorm produced two tornadoes starting just over the state line up in the western piedmont of North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a couple reports of half dollar size hail from the south side of Simpsonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe storms moved into the Upstate and northwest piedmont during the mid afternoon hours. Additional storms developed over the western Upstate along merging outflow boundaries from earlier storms. A particularly intense storm affected Anderson on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball size size hail fell on Fox Squirrel Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was present over South Carolina as an area of low pressure remained west of the county warning area with a weak surface front extending east along the Savannah River. Precipitable water values approached 2.5 inches as tropical moisture continued to advect over the region from the south during the late morning into early afternoon hours. As an early afternoon sea breeze developed, high precipitable water values contributed to several showers and thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water entering the crawl space of a residence at 1261 Mathis Ferry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow overspread the region allowing tropical moisture originating in the Caribbean Sea to stream into southern South Carolina.  A developing area of low pressure over the nearby Atlantic Coastal Waters and a deepening upper level low west of the region, resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and local broadcast media reported the yards of several residences were flooded as well as water flowing across some roadways around McClellanville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree, causing it to fall on a mobile home.  Three people were inside, but were not injurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse storms developed over much of the Midlands producing damaging winds and large hail.  Many trees were taken down in some areas along with powerlines.  Golfballs size hail was observed in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lugoff FD reported 3/4 inch hail northwest of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Microburst from scattered thunderstorms produced wind damage across the central Midlands and Pee Dee region...mainly from trees being taken down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported trees down on Highways 32 and 52.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that was approaching the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter to half dollar size hail along with some large branches down near Hwy. 21 and Interstate 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms in the Midlands produced wind damage and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported numerous trees down at Hwy 178 and Fairview Road and also at I-20 exit 39.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across the region during the afternoon hours. Afternoon temperatures ranged from 95 to 100 degrees with dewpoint temperatures in the 70s. This produce a moderately to strongly unstable airmass. There was enough low level moisture for scattered showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff dispatch reported a tree down at the intersection of Highway 378 and Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon ahead of a strong shortwave.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record heat across the area along with plenty of moisture produced heat index values between 115 and 125 degrees.  Surface Based CAPE values reached as high as 6000-6500 j/kg with DCAPE values approaching 1500-1600 j/kg.  Thunderstorms initially formed in close proximity to an inland trough of low pressure and along the sea breeze.  The convection sent out numerous outflow boundaries triggering additional thunderstorm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down off of Longridge Road, 3 miles west-southwest of Murraysville, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled across the county warning area producing more wet microburst that took down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported trees down on Calhoun Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in the heat and humidity of the afternoon. There were no reports of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small hail fell along Carolina Forest Boulevard. The public report was relayed through local television media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms took down some trees with microbursts and also produced flash flooding in Columbia.  Heavy rains also produced flooding in some low lying areas around the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol and DOT reported several trees down along I-95 and Hwy 301 southwest of Manning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed over the Upstate and northwest Piedmont of South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours as a cold front moved into the region. Several of the storms produced wind damage along with a couple reports of hail.
